Title: Slack → Teams
What's the same: Channels, DMs, threads, file sharing, status, integrations, search
Gotchas: Slack threads are first-class; Teams uses channel posts + replies which feels different. Slack = one workspace; Teams = many teams within a tenant. Slack Huddle ↔ Teams "Meet now". Slack Canvas ↔ Loop component.

Title: Google Chat → Teams
What's the same: Spaces, DMs, threads, files in chat, search
Gotchas: Google Chat Spaces are lighter weight than Teams (no embedded SharePoint site). Google's threading is conversation-style; Teams is post-with-replies. Less integration density than Teams.

Title: Mattermost → Teams
What's the same: Channels, DMs, threads, slash commands, integrations, self-hostable
Gotchas: Mattermost is open-source and federated; Teams is Microsoft-managed cloud. Slash commands ↔ Teams bot commands or Power Automate actions. Mattermost playbooks ↔ Power Automate flows.

Title: Discord → Teams
What's the same: Channels, voice rooms, threads, roles, file sharing
Gotchas: Discord servers ↔ Teams. Voice channels (always-on) ↔ Teams "Meet now" (ad-hoc). Roles + permissions are richer in Discord; Teams uses M365 group membership.

Title: Slack Huddles → Teams Meet now
What's the same: Quick voice meeting in a channel without a calendar invite
Gotchas: Teams "Meet now" is started from the channel header; Huddles are inline. Both support screenshare. Teams call recording is more enterprise-friendly.

Title: Slack Canvas → Loop component
What's the same: Live-editable shared doc inside a chat
Gotchas: Loop components live across Teams chat, Outlook, Word, Whiteboard. Canvas is Slack-only. Loop has more block types (tables, voting, task lists).

Title: Slack Workflow Builder → Power Automate
What's the same: No-code visual automation with triggers and actions
Gotchas: Power Automate has 1000+ connectors (vs. Slack's much smaller set). Power Automate runs anywhere in M365; Workflow Builder only inside Slack. Power Automate has approval flows + Adaptive Cards.

Title: Google Drive (My Drive) → OneDrive
What's the same: Personal cloud files, sharing links, version history, search, sync client
Gotchas: OneDrive is strictly personal; team files belong in SharePoint. Drive blends personal + shared. Permissions in OneDrive are simpler links; SharePoint uses site groups for team files.

Title: Google Drive (Shared Drive) → SharePoint document library
What's the same: Team-owned files that survive personnel changes, granular permissions
Gotchas: SharePoint libraries live inside SharePoint sites and have richer metadata (columns, content types). Permissions inherit from the site by default. "Shared with me" lives in different places (OneDrive vs SharePoint).

Title: Google Docs → Word
What's the same: Real-time collaboration, comments, suggestions, version history, share links
Gotchas: Word for the Web ≈ Google Docs UX. Word desktop has more advanced layout features. Track Changes (Word) ≈ Suggesting mode (Docs). Docs is browser-native; Word can fall back to desktop app.

Title: Google Sheets → Excel
What's the same: Cells, formulas, charts, pivot tables, conditional formatting, real-time collab
Gotchas: Excel formulas are deeper (LAMBDA, dynamic arrays, Power Query). Sheets is lighter, faster on simple tasks. Excel + Power Query ↔ Sheets + Apps Script for data transformation.

Title: Google Slides → PowerPoint
What's the same: Slides, themes, animations, real-time collab, presenter view
Gotchas: PowerPoint has richer animations and design themes. Slides is faster to load. Designer (PowerPoint AI) ↔ similar feature in Slides.

Title: Gmail → Outlook
What's the same: Email, labels/folders, filters/rules, search, signatures
Gotchas: Gmail labels ↔ Outlook folders + categories (subtle: a message in Gmail can have multiple labels; in Outlook it's in one folder + can have multiple categories). Outlook has Focused Inbox and richer rules.

Title: Google Calendar → Outlook Calendar
What's the same: Events, recurring meetings, invitees, RSVP, multiple calendars, time zone handling
Gotchas: Outlook tightly integrated with Teams (every meeting can become a Teams meeting). Find Time / Scheduling Assistant in Outlook ≈ Find a Time in Calendar. Outlook recurring patterns are more complex.

Title: Google Forms → Microsoft Forms
What's the same: Multiple question types, response collection, share links, basic logic
Gotchas: Microsoft Forms has fewer question types but tighter Teams + Power Automate integration. Forms responses can route to a List or Excel via Power Automate. Branching logic is similar.

Title: Google Sites → SharePoint Site / Pages
What's the same: Build internal pages with text, images, embedded content
Gotchas: SharePoint pages are richer (web parts, news, hero, embedded Lists, Power BI). Sites is simpler/easier. Permissions in SharePoint are tied to site membership; Sites uses link-share or specific users.

Title: Google Keep → OneNote
What's the same: Quick notes, lists, reminders, color-coding, search, mobile capture
Gotchas: OneNote has Notebooks → Sections → Pages structure (much deeper). Keep is flat with labels. OneNote is better for long-form; Keep is better for quick capture.

Title: Apps Script → Power Automate
What's the same: Automate across the suite (email, calendar, docs, sheets) with triggers
Gotchas: Apps Script is JavaScript code; Power Automate is no-code visual flows (Power Fx for advanced). Apps Script has deeper Workspace integration; Power Automate has 1000+ connectors. Both have time-driven and event-driven triggers.

Title: Google Tasks → Microsoft To Do
What's the same: Personal task list with due dates, simple subtasks, reminders
Gotchas: To Do has lists, Today view, smart suggestions, and integration with flagged emails. Google Tasks is bare-bones by comparison. Both sync to mobile.

Title: Asana → Planner
What's the same: Tasks, due dates, assignees, boards, labels, project structure
Gotchas: Asana has subtasks + dependencies + multiple project views (list/board/timeline) built in. Planner is simpler. "Project for the Web" (paid) closes the gap. Asana has richer reporting.

Title: Trello → Planner
What's the same: Kanban boards, cards, lists/buckets, labels, due dates, attachments
Gotchas: Planner buckets ≈ Trello lists. Trello Power-Ups (extensions) don't have a direct equivalent — use Power Automate flows instead. Trello is simpler for solo use; Planner integrates tightly with Teams + Outlook.

Title: Jira → Azure DevOps Boards
What's the same: Issue tracking, sprints, backlogs, workflows, custom fields
Gotchas: Azure DevOps is the closest analog for engineering teams. For project coordinator use, Planner is usually enough. Jira's workflow customization is much deeper than either.

Title: Notion → Loop + OneNote
What's the same: Docs + databases + wikis in one tool with blocks
Gotchas: Loop = the live components piece. OneNote = the long-form notebook piece. SharePoint Lists = the database piece. No single Microsoft app replicates all three. Notion's all-in-one is genuinely unique.

Title: Looker Studio → Power BI
What's the same: Connect data sources, build interactive reports, share, embed
Gotchas: Power BI is more powerful (DAX, semantic models, Power Query) and integrates natively with M365. Looker Studio is free and easier for quick reports. Power BI Pro license required to share/embed.

Title: Google Meet → Teams Meetings
What's the same: Video meetings, screenshare, recording, captions, breakout rooms
Gotchas: Teams meetings have richer enterprise features (Together mode, PowerPoint Live, whiteboard, Loop in meeting). Meet is faster to start ad-hoc. Both support phone dial-in.

Title: Zoom → Teams Meetings
What's the same: Video meetings, recording, breakouts, registration
Gotchas: Teams replaces Zoom for most internal use; Zoom often kept for external partners. Recording goes to OneDrive (Teams) vs Zoom Cloud. Teams breakout rooms work well; Zoom historically had richer settings.

Title: Google Workspace Admin → M365 Admin Center
What's the same: Manage users, groups, licenses, security, mobile devices
Gotchas: M365 admin is split across multiple consoles (M365 Admin, Entra/Azure AD, Intune, Compliance, Purview, Security). Workspace Admin is one console. Steeper learning curve in M365.

Title: Google Groups → Microsoft 365 Groups
What's the same: Mailing lists, shared inboxes, permissions group
Gotchas: M365 Groups bundle a mailbox + calendar + SharePoint site + Teams team + Planner — much more than a mailing list. Workspace Groups are simpler. Distribution Lists ≠ M365 Groups; both exist in M365.

Title: Google SSO → Entra ID (Azure AD)
What's the same: Single sign-on, app authorization, MFA, conditional access
Gotchas: Entra has more granular conditional access policies. Both can federate with each other. SAML/OIDC standards make migration mechanical.

Title: Slack /remind → (no native equivalent)
What's the same: Schedule a future reminder for yourself or a channel
Gotchas: No direct "/remind" in Teams. Closest: Power Automate scheduled flow that posts to a channel, OR Microsoft To Do reminder on a flagged email, OR a Planner task with due date. Friction is real.

Title: Loop component → Slack Canvas
What's the same: Live-editable shared block in chat
Gotchas: Reverse mapping: if you're going Microsoft → Google then Loop has no real Google equivalent (Canvas is Slack-only). If your Google org uses Slack you have Canvas; otherwise nothing.
